examples.title: Linear Search vs Binary Search in Sequential Files
examples.description: Comparison of search algorithms for sequential file organization
examples.code:
// Linear Search - O(n)
FUNCTION linearSearch(file, key)
  WHILE NOT EOF(file)
    READ record
    IF record.key == key THEN
      RETURN record
    END IF
  END WHILE
  RETURN NOT FOUND
END FUNCTION

// Binary Search - O(log n) - requires sorted file
FUNCTION binarySearch(file, key)
  left = 0
  right = file.numRecords - 1
  
  WHILE left <= right DO
    mid = (left + right) / 2
    READ record at mid
    
    IF record.key == key THEN
      RETURN record
    ELSE IF record.key < key THEN
      left = mid + 1
    ELSE
      right = mid - 1
    END IF
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN NOT FOUND
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Sequential files support both linear and binary search. Binary search requires sorted order and provides O(log n) complexity but needs random access capability.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for linear, O(log n) for binary

examples.title: B+ Tree Insertion Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for inserting a key into a B+ tree
examples.explanation: B+ tree insertion maintains balance by splitting nodes when they overflow. Keys are duplicated to parent nodes for routing, while all data remains at leaf level.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per level, O(log n) total

examples.title: Hash Index Implementation
examples.description: Simple hash index with chaining for collision handling
examples.code:
// Hash Index with Chaining
CONSTANT NUM_BUCKETS = 10

STRUCTURE HashIndex
  buckets[NUM_BUCKETS]  // Array of linked lists
END STRUCTURE

// Hash function
FUNCTION hashFunction(key)
  RETURN key MOD NUM_BUCKETS
END FUNCTION

// Insert into hash index
FUNCTION hashInsert(index, key, recordPointer)
  bucketNum = hashFunction(key)
  
  // Check if key already exists
  node = index.buckets[bucketNum]
  WHILE node != NULL DO
    IF node.key == key THEN
      node.recordPointer = recordPointer  // Update
      RETURN
    END IF
    node = node.next
  END WHILE
  
  // Insert at beginning of chain
  newNode = allocateNode()
  newNode.key = key
  newNode.recordPointer = recordPointer
  newNode.next = index.buckets[bucketNum]
  index.buckets[bucketNum] = newNode
END FUNCTION

// Search in hash index
FUNCTION hashSearch(index, key)
  bucketNum = hashFunction(key)
  node = index.buckets[bucketNum]
  
  WHILE node != NULL DO
    IF node.key == key THEN
      RETURN node.recordPointer
    END IF
    node = node.next
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN NULL  // Not found
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Hash index uses a hash function to compute bucket addresses directly. Chaining handles collisions by maintaining linked lists at each bucket. Provides O(1) average case lookup.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average, O(n) worst case with many collisions
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing all entries plus O(b) for buckets
